rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Worktree mechanics, per-cwd session boundary, gitignored-files-don't-cross-by-git all match compendium §6d / §7b / §7c shipped reality.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Sessions are the unit of work — write 'start a new session in X'.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Body uses 'Start a new Claude Code session in your repo' / 'Start a second Claude Code session in another window' — correct sessions-as-unit framing.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Cross-cwd boundaries always open `new`; evidence travels by disk.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Sibling worktree fork = different cwd = new session. Body names personal files (`CLAUDE.local.md`, `.claude/memory/`) don't sync by git — copy at fork time + diverge independently. Exact mapping to §7b.

rules_evaluated.evidence: L58: 'Worktree-as-side-quest framing inherited from Boris Cherny, *Mastering Claude Code in 30 minutes* (parallel worktrees as productivity unlock).' — named talk, no URL.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Maintainer-block 'Frameworks riffed on' attribution to a specific named talk normally takes a URL when natural. Non-load-bearing (no number/quote being cited) — TODO not blocker. Either add URL (YouTube / event page) or note as `[cultural-vocab]` if treating as community-vocabulary level.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Source-verification maintainer block on any student-facing file with practitioner citations.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: No load-bearing practitioner number/quote in student-facing body; maintainer-block framework attribution is acknowledgement, not load-bearing citation. Section not required.

`<repo-name>` tokens inside the fenced prompt are agent-resolved variables (Claude reads its cwd folder name and substitutes), not student fill-ins. Body line above the prompt explicitly tells Claude how to resolve them. Per rule 32's exemptions for branch/worktree-path identifiers in backticks, the angle-bracket convention is acceptable here.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Every prompt block gets a one-sentence action lead-in.
rules_evaluated.evidence: L15: 'Paste the prompt as-is. Claude reads `<repo-name>` as your repo's folder name from the working directory.' and L23: 'Start a second Claude Code session in another window, in the sibling worktree directory Claude just named...' — neither is a one-sentence command-verb summary of what the prompt does.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Add a semantic action lead-in for each prompt — e.g., 'Ask Claude to fork a sibling worktree at the default path and copy your personal files across.' for the first; 'Ask Claude to confirm the worktree state and wait.' for the second. Body context already orients the student to the move; the lead-in is the skim-cue rule 2 asks for.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Canonical presentation shape — lead-in, label with destination, blank line, fenced block.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Expander reconstructs canonical `**Prompt** *(Claude Code)*` shape per registry frontmatter. Verified in expand-md.js output.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Installed Claude Code Skills invoke by name, never by path.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: No skill invocations in either prompt body.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Chain prompts by back-reference and deterministic path.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Registry frontmatter on `ae101-m3-quality-side-orient` declares `requires: m3-quality-worktree` from `ae101-m3-fork-quality-side`. First prompt reports the absolute worktree path; second-prompt body in the exercise says 'in the sibling worktree directory Claude just named' (back-reference). Chain mechanics sound.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Markdown shout inside fenced blocks is dead weight.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: No bold/italic markdown inside either fenced block. Bullets use plain hyphens. Backticks used only for path strings (acceptable as quote-marks per rule 9).
AE101-scoped exercise — rule 23 carve-out explicitly exempts AE101 (student's existing repo as cwd, no training-imposed structure).

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Don't restate session CWD inside the prompt body.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Carve-out applies: the first prompt's intent IS to change/create a working directory (worktree fork), so explicit paths inside the fence are the action, not bookkeeping.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Prompts live in the registry; module/exercise files reference them by marker.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Both prompts properly migrated to `curriculum/prompts/ae101-m3-fork-quality-side.md` and `curriculum/prompts/ae101-m3-quality-side-orient.md`; exercise references by `{{prompt:<key>}}` markers.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: No module-name references in prompt prose.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Neither prompt body contains bare M-tokens. Exercise body's 'Module 3' mentions are in student-facing prose, not in the fenced prompts.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Declare permission-mode when the paste requires a specific Claude Code mode.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: Neither prompt depends on plan/acceptEdits mode. Default-mode paste; no chip required. Frontmatter correctly omits the field.

rules_evaluated.rule_lead: Artifact-realism — every named tool/skill/connector must exist.
rules_evaluated.fix_hint: `git worktree add` is a real git subcommand. `~/.claude/skills/test-strategy/SKILL.md` is forward-named — the student authors it in M3 Ex3, which the body explicitly states. No artifact-existence violation.
Second prompt IS the smoke-test for the worktree fork — confirms cwd / branch / loaded rules / codebase visibility before downstream M3 work depends on it.
Second prompt explicitly checks whether `./CLAUDE.md` and `./CLAUDE.local.md` auto-loaded — surface-running the auto-load tier, consistent with the staged-progression principle.

platform_claims_checked.claim: git worktree add makes a sibling folder on its own branch
Personal files (CLAUDE.local.md, .claude/memory/) don't sync between worktrees
both sessions see the same git history but keep their own working state
skills installed to user scope cross worktrees freely
skills at ~/.claude/skills/<name>/ auto-discovered every session regardless of working directory

citations_checked.claim: Boris Cherny, Mastering Claude Code in 30 minutes — parallel worktrees as productivity unlock
